engineering.translation: The chapter sharpens the equation. What you have been calling renunciation is yoga; the two names point at the same orientation. The cut is at saṅkalpa — the desire-laden imaginings about results — not at action itself. Without abandoning these saṅkalpa-fancies no engineer becomes a yogi. The verse names something specific: the rehearsals you run in your head before the deploy, the mental preview of the dashboard reading, the imagined slack-thread of executives congratulating you, the imagined recruiter reaching out after the launch. Those imaginings are saṅkalpa. The engineer who has cut these has cut what 6.1 demanded; the engineer who has not has merely renamed their fruit-dependence as ambition. There is no path that bypasses the cut. The verse is operationally precise — neither path lets you keep the imaginings.
engineering.concrete_scenario: A senior engineer at a fintech is preparing the announcement post for a Stripe-webhook reliability rewrite he has spent four months on. He notices, while drafting the GitHub release notes, that he has rehearsed the executive-Slack reaction six times this morning: the CTO's reply, the VP-Eng's bookmark, the DM from the recruiter who tried to poach him in February. He notices a second loop: the imagined response of his ex-manager, who had pushed back on the rewrite. The post is good; the rewrite is good; the launch is going to be fine. The saṅkalpa is what is not fine. He drafts the announcement, posts it, closes the laptop, walks to lunch. Three weeks later, when the dashboard does eventually show the latency improvement, he is half-surprised — he had not been refreshing it. That is the cut. The verse does not require him to feel nothing about the work; it requires him to stop running the rehearsals about its reception.
engineering.falsifiability: The analog fails if a reader hears 'cut saṅkalpa' as 'never imagine the future.' Engineering planning explicitly requires forecasting outcomes (capacity planning, latency targets, rollout plans). The cut is at the desire-laden imaginings about the reception of the work, not at the operational forecasting of system behavior. An engineer who concludes that they should stop writing design docs has misread the verse.
engineering.counter_example: When an engineer is doing legitimate forecasting — what a system will do under 10x traffic, what error budgets to set, what the rollback looks like — that is not saṅkalpa. The verse cuts at the loop that runs about you, not at the loop that runs about the system. A counterexample: the engineer who has cut their reception-rehearsals but still does rigorous capacity planning is exactly what the verse describes; the verse is not anti-foresight.
engineering.implication: Notice when you are running a saṅkalpa-loop. Distinguish system-forecasting from reception-rehearsing. Cut the second; keep the first. The chapter's later instruction (6.10-6.32) presupposes you have done this cut.
engineering.quotable_line: Renunciation IS yoga; without cutting the imagined-reception loop, no engineer becomes a yogi.

iterations.mutation: v0 generation: BG 6.2 engineering layer drafted from triangulated source pack with anchoring to chapters/06-thesis.md. Chapter 6 — the cut at saṅkalpa, with concrete naming of the rehearsal-loop.
